Chunghwa Precision Test Tech Co Ltd :

data.title: Euro zone bond yields steady ahead of U.S. inflation data
data.description:
Euro zone government bond yields were little changed on Friday but were set to notch weekly gains as investors turned their focus to the release of the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge later in the day.

Upbeat U.S. economic data on Thursday triggered a selloff in Treasuries, pushing the benchmark 10-year yield up nearly 5 basis points to 4.1950% — its highest in three weeks, as traders scaled back their bets on a rate cut from the Fed next month.

By Friday, Germany's 10-year bond yield (DE10YT=RR), the benchmark for the euro zone, was steady at 2.7764%, having ended Thursday's session up 2.5 basis points.

On Friday, the 10-year Treasury yield was up another 1 basis point at 4.1872%, while the 30-year yield (US30YT=RR) added 1.9 bps to 4.7724%.

The personal consumption expenditures (PCE) price index, due later on Friday, is expected to provide a clearer steer on the Fed's policy outlook.

In Europe, French unions are planning fresh protests on October 2 to pressure new Prime Minister Sebastien Lecornu into shelving a fiscal austerity programme.

France's 10-year yield (FR10YT=RR) was flat at 3.6012%, leaving the gap between German and French yields — a closely watched risk premium — wider at 82.54 bps.

Italy's 10-year bond yield was also stable at 3.6439%. On the long end, Germany's 30-year yield (DE30YT=RR) inched up 1 bp to 3.3573%.

data.title: Trump says South Korea, Japan will pay billions 'upfront' in investment
data.description:
Key points:
  • Trump says South Korea, Japan would pay combined $900 billion
  • Seoul official says funds to be disbursed per project, not in lump sum
  • Japan-U.S. deal provides for investments "from time to time"

U.S. President Donald Trump insisted that South Korea would provide billions of dollars in investments "upfront", despite Seoul's contention that it would be plunged into a financial crisis if it met the U.S. demands without safeguards.

Trump's remarks contradict South Korea's understanding of its trade deal with the United States, however, a government official told Reuters, speaking on condition of anonymity because of the sensitivity of the talks.

"We have never thought about making the investments in lump sum," the South Korean official added, adding that both South Korea and Japan intended to provide financing for projects only after a "capital call" made by the United States.

South Korea, which pledged $350 billion toward U.S. projects in July, has balked at U.S. demands for control over the funds and South Korean officials say talks to formalise their trade deal are at a deadlock.

Trump formalised a trade deal with Japan this month, lowering tariffs on imports of its automobiles and other products in return for $550 billion of its investment in U.S. projects, and U.S. officials have pressed Seoul to follow suit.

"We have in Japan it's $550 billion, South Korea's $350 billion. That's upfront," Trump told reporters on Thursday in the Oval Office, as he touted the amount of money he said his sweeping tariffs have brought in.

Trump's comments came as political doubts have increasingly dogged his trade talks with South Korea, spooking investors who now worry Seoul may end up with a raw deal or perhaps none at all.

Both Japan and South Korea have said they will make investments based on U.S. projects, rather than paying the total of $900 billion upfront.

A memorandum of understanding on Japan's $550 billion investment agreed with the United States in September also made no mention of "upfront" payment of the funds.

It says the investments should be made 'from time to time' until the end of Trump's term in January 2029. Under its deal, Tokyo agreed to transfer money within 45 days after the U.S. selected a project.

Japanese officials did not comment on Trump's "upfront" remarks on Friday.

South Korea has also said it cannot afford to make large cash investments. Last week President Lee Jae Myung told Reuters that without safeguards such as a currency swap, South Korea's economy could be plunged into crisis.

A second South Korean government official declined to comment on Trump's remarks, but reiterated that talks with the United States were based on the principle that the deal should meet national interests and be commercially feasible.

Analysts say a currency swap is unlikely, and South Korean negotiators are pushing for most of the funds to be in the form of loans, rather than direct investment.

They are also pressing Washington for mechanisms to ensure that the projects are commercially viable.

data.title: Gulf Keystone Petroleum, Genel Energy rise on Kurdish crude export resumption
data.description:
** UK-listed Kurdish oil producer Gulf Keystone Petroleum rises 4.32% to 217.5p, highest since October 2022

** Kurdistan-focused oil firm Genel Energy gains as much as 4.95% to 78.8p

** Group of international oil companies sign agreements with Kurdistan Regional Government and Federal Government of Iraq to restart international crude exports from Kurdistan

** GKP expects Shaikan Field realised prices to improve to above $30/bbl in interim period vs $27-28/bbl in local sales

** GKP's gains ~27%, GENL up ~2% this year, as of last close

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
Market Talks covering the impact of U.S. Politics and White House policies on companies and markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0337 ET - European pharmaceutical stocks fell in early trade after President Trump announced 100% tariffs on those companies that aren't building plants in the U.S. Swiss skincare company Galderma was the biggest faller, losing 3.55%, while Novo Nordisk shares are down 1.3%. AstraZeneca, Roche and Bayer shares were all down 0.2%. Tariffs are expected to start from Oct. 1.(ian.walker@wsj.com)

0318 ET - President Trump's announcement of 25% tariffs on imported heavy trucks is light on detail, but Daimler Truck could suffer the most given current production in the U.S. and overall exposure to the U.S. at the group level, Bernstein analysts write. U.S. manufacturer Paccar could be the greatest beneficiary, it adds. Bernstein notes the tariffs apply from Oct. 1, so they won't impact the third quarter, where orders and volumes are weak for all manufacturers. It adds that further inflationary increases to truck prices will negatively impact all, especially with new truck prices currently considerably elevated after many years of price increases. It rates Volvo and Daimler Truck at underperform and Traton at market-perform. Volvo shares rise 2.1%, Daimler Truck shares fall 4.4% and Traton shares drop 2.1%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0301 ET - The dollar turns lower after President Trump announced a new round of tariffs, including a 100% tariff on drugs by companies that aren't building plants in the U.S. as well as levies on large trucks and home goods, beginning Oct. 1. The dollar had jumped to a three-week high against a basket of currencies on Thursday after a string of strong U.S. economic data, including a sizeable upgrade to U.S. second-quarter GDP. This damped prospects of interest-rate cuts. However, Commerzbank analyst Michael Pfister says the U.S. economy's dependence on private consumption poses a risk if sentiment declines. Trump's "erratic trade policy is doing much to dampen consumer sentiment," he says. The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 98.409 after hitting a high of 98.605 on Thursday. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0300 ET - The market likely doesn't believe the U.S.'s pharmaceutical tariffs will have real, significant bite in the end, says Citi Wealth's Ken Peng in a media roundtable. The head of Asia investment strategy notes President Trump offered an "escape" for pharma manufacturers, as they will be exempted from the tariffs as long as they start to build a plant in the U.S. While Asian pharma names fell after the news, Peng says: "If people really believed that large swaths of branded pharma would be tariffed at 100%, [the shares' movement] wouldn't be a 3%-4% correction... I think the market is calling it bluff." The new tariffs are also unlikely to hurt U.S.-China trade negotiations much, which he views as more important. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0239 ET - The impact of U.S. medical-device tariffs on European med-tech companies hinges on whether products for disabled people will be exempted, Bernstein analysts write. Products for disabled people are exempted from tariffs under the Nairobi Protocol and the effect of tariffs on the sector will depend on whether levies can override it, according to Bernstein. If applicable, chronic-care specialists including Coloplast and ConvaTec would be more exposed to potential levies than hearing-aid makers including Sonova, Demant, GN Store Nord and Amplifon, the analysts say. Another critical factor is manufacturing location. A recent U.S.-EU trade agreement caps tariffs on medicines at 15%, but it is unclear if similar agreements would shield medical technologies, Bernstein writes. Convatec fell 5.6%, Demant 4.8%, and GN 6.6% on Thursday. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0121 ET - The impact of U.S. trade policy on Japan's corporate profits, investment and wage growth could be limited if Japanese companies continue serving U.S. customers through their local subsidiaries, Capital Economics says. While BOJ data suggest higher U.S. tariffs have weighed on Japanese exports, the September manufacturing PMI subindex for new export orders indicates export growth is unlikely to weaken much further, notes Asia-Pacific head Marcel Thieliant. He adds that Japanese companies are already mitigating tariffs by ramping up production in their U.S. subsidiaries, with Japan's U.S.-bound FDI on track to hit a record high this year. However, Thieliant suggests that this strength in FDI likely reflects U.S. economic outperformance rather than trade-policy changes. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0030 ET - After a period of calm on the tariff front, U.S. President Trump announces another round of sectoral tariffs to take effect Oct. 1. Gauging the impact of the pharmaceutical tariffs is difficult, as many big drug companies already have U.S. facilities and have announced expansions, says Shane Oliver, chief economist at AMP. But the further tariffs, with more still to come on products like semiconductors, highlight that the tariff threat has not gone away, he adds. The latest announcements take the average U.S. tariff rate before any substitution to around 21%, well down from post-Liberation Day highs but well up from about 2.5% at the start of the year, Oliver says. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

2003 ET - A quick finalization of South Korea's $350 billion U.S. investment fund deal in exchange for lowering U.S. tariffs is less likely, says Citigroup analyst Jin-Wook Kim in a note. Seoul and Washington have struggled to work out details of the deal, verbally agreed on in July but yet to be formally signed. Both sides could either reach a partial compromise after a prolonged deadlock or see the U.S. impose an earlier proposed 25% or even higher tariff on Korean goods if they fail to reach a final agreement during the Oct. 31-Nov. 1 APEC Korea Summit, which Trump is reportedly attending, Kim says. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

1843 ET - Costco Wholesale executives commented that its non-food category is experiencing the biggest increase in inflation. In a call with analysts, management said non-food items have been deflationary for about 12 months, but ticked up by the low-single digits in the fiscal third quarter. As a result, Costco recorded a $43 million last-in, first-out charge from inflation. "We'd continued to see inflation in food and fresh, but that was being offset by non-foods," Chief Financial Officer Gary Millerchip said. Costco is leaning on domestically produced and Kirkland-brand items to keep down prices for items like mattresses and tires.(katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1344 ET - CBOT grain futures pare back gains as agitation around China's absence from the U.S. grain export market grows. News of Argentina reinstating export taxes on agricultural shipments gave grains an early boost, but now some groups are voicing disappointment over how the U.S. government handled the situation. In a statement, American Soybean Association president Caleb Ragland criticized the move by Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent to extend credit to Argentina, claiming that it enabled China to again bypass the U.S. for its soy needs. "The farm economy is suffering while our competitors supplant the United States," says Ragland. Soybeans are up 0.1%, while corn climbs 0.3% and wheat rises 1.3%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1254 ET - Soybeans get some support from a Reuters report quoting a spokesperson for China's commerce ministry saying that the U.S. would need to remove "unreasonable tariffs" in order for China to resume purchasing U.S. soybeans. Grain traders and analysts appear to be taking this as a signal that China could again engage the U.S. market. But at the same time, China purchased a large number of soybeans from Argentina this week, displacing what is usually a purchase of U.S. soybeans, says Doug Bergman of RCM Alternatives in a note. CBOT soybeans are flat, while corn is up 0.2% and wheat rises 1.2%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0959 ET - - A colder weather forecast and risks of disruptions to Russian supplies push European natural-gas prices higher, with the benchmark Dutch TTF contract up 2.1% to 32.59 euros a megawatt hour in afternoon trade. Storage levels across the bloc are 82% full, above the Nov. 1 target, while Norwegian gas exports increased after weeks of maintenance works. "The region has been successful in meeting goals to refill storage levels," ANZ analysts say. "However, they remain lower than in previous years." Meanwhile, Ukrainian attacks on Russian energy infrastructure, the latest EU sanctions package and President Trump's calls for European countries to stop buying Russian energy provide underlying support to prices. According to DNB, investment funds increased their net positioning in TTF by 2 terawatt hours last week, which means the funds are holding more long positions, expecting a rise in prices. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

data.title: European Pharmaceutical Stocks Fall On New Trump Tariffs — Market Talk
data.description: European pharmaceutical stocks fell in early trade after President Trump announced 100% tariffs on those companies that aren't building plants in the U.S. Swiss skincare company Galderma was the biggest faller, losing 3.55%, while Novo Nordisk shares are down 1.3%. AstraZeneca, Roche and Bayer shares were all down 0.2%. Tariffs are expected to start from Oct. 1.(ian.walker@wsj.com)

data.title: India stocks, rupee, bonds, swaps, call at 1:05 p.m. IST
data.description:
STOCKS:

The benchmark BSE Sensex lost 0.4% to 80,833 and the broader NSE Nifty 50 index declined 0.4% to 24,082, driven by losses in pharmaceutical shares after U.S. President Donald Trump announced a 100% levy on branded and patented drugs.

RUPEE:

The Indian rupee inched lower versus the U.S. dollar at 88.70, after U.S. President Donald Trump unveiled a new round of tariffs, including a 100% duty on any branded or patented pharmaceutical products.

GOVERNMENT BONDS:

The benchmark 10-year bond (IN063335G=CC) was quoted at 98.85 rupees, with the yield little changed at 6.4910%, as markets awaited debt auction result.

OVERNIGHT INDEX SWAPS:

The one-year overnight index swap rate (INR1YMIBROIS=CC) dipped marginally to 5.45%, while the five-year swap rate (INR5YMIBROIS=CC) inched up to 5.73%.

CALL MONEY:

India's overnight call money rate (INROND=) was at 5.15% and the overnight TREPS rate (INTREPTOT=TCCL) was at 5.45%.

data.title: PRESSR: Alvarez & Marsal’s Global Transaction Advisory Group continues expansion in Middle East
data.description:
Abu Dhabi – Leading global professional services firm Alvarez & Marsal’s (A&M) Global Transaction Advisory Group (Global TAG) announces the appointment of Uthman Al-Basri as Managing Director in Abu Dhabi, as Global TAG’s EMEA unit continues to build its footprint in the Middle East.

Mr. Al-Basri brings over 15 years of experience advising clients on landmark transactions, including the creation of First Abu Dhabi Bank (FAB), Abu Dhabi Commercial Bank (ADCB), Saudi National Bank (SNB), and Saudi Awwal Bank (SAB). Specializing in financial services, his expertise spans buy-side and sell-side financial due diligence, vendor diligence, sale and purchase agreement (SPA) support, and capital markets readiness. Mr. Al-Basri has also led cross-border acquisitions in Turkey, North Africa, and Central Asia, as well as major insurance M&A transactions in Saudi Arabia. Prior to joining A&M, he was a Partner in KPMG’s Middle East transaction services practice and previously led financial services M&A engagements at Deloitte.

Paul Aversano, Managing Director and Global Practice Leader of Global TAG, commented: “As EMEA continues to attract global capital and an influx of cross-border M&A activity, TAG EMEA is at the ready. Our structural nimbleness and integrated solutions model allow us to more quickly assess and address evolving market needs – it’s what sets A&M apart. The appointment of Uthman demonstrates our continued commitment to listen intently to the market – one that calls for expanding our capabilities and local footprint in key economic hubs, all with the lens to help our clients further unlock cross-border deal value while mitigating risk in an increasing complex market.”

Ali Anwar, Managing Director and Middle East Practice Leader of A&M’s Global Transaction Advisory Group in Dubai, added:

 “The Middle East remains one of the world’s most dynamic investment destinations, fueled by sustained economic growth, ambitious diversification agendas, and a fast-evolving regulatory landscape. Uthman’s appointment reinforces our commitment to expanding A&M’s senior-led presence in the region, ensuring we are at the forefront of advising clients on complex, high-profile transactions and helping them capture opportunities across the GCC and beyond.” 

Commenting on his appointment, Mr. Al-Basri noted: “The Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) is home to some of the world’s most active financial services markets, with increasing complexity across banking, insurance, asset management, and fintech transactions. A&M’s integrated model, built on deep functional and industry expertise, is a natural fit for purpose for meeting the market’s current and future M&A demands.”

About Alvarez & Marsal

Founded in 1983, Alvarez & Marsal is a leading global professional services firm. Renowned for its leadership, action and results, Alvarez & Marsal provides advisory, business performance improvement and turnaround management services, delivering practical solutions to address clients' unique challenges. With a world-wide network of experienced operators, world-class consultants, former regulators and industry authorities, Alvarez & Marsal helps corporates, boards, private equity firms, law firms and government agencies drive transformation, mitigate risk and unlock value at every stage of growth.

About Alvarez & Marsal Global Transaction Advisory Group

A&M’s Global Transaction Advisory Group provides investors and lenders the answers needed to get the deal done. We combine our firm’s deep operational, industry and functional resources with Big Four-quality financial accounting and tax expertise to assess key deal drivers and focus on the root cause of any critical deal issues. As the largest transaction advisory practice outside the Big Four, our global integrated teams help private equity, sovereign wealth funds, family offices and hedge funds as well as corporate acquirers unlock value across the investment lifecycle.

The firm’s Global Transaction Advisory Group includes over 1,200 professionals in 45 offices worldwide. Our global team has extensive industry knowledge across multiple sectors and is free from audit-based conflicts of interest.

Send us your press releases to pressrelease.zawya@lseg.com

data.title: Safran to Benefit From Turkish Airlines' Boeing Aircraft Purchase — Market Talk
data.description: Safran will get a boost from Turkish Airlines' Boeing aircraft purchase, as the French manufacturer produces the Leap engine for the 737 MAX and equipment for the 787 plane, Jefferies analysts say. Turkish Airlines said Friday that it decided to purchase a total of 75 aircraft from Boeing, consisting of 50 firm and 25 option orders for 787-9 and 787-10 models with deliveries scheduled between 2029 and 2034. Negotiations with Boeing regarding the purchase of a total of 150 aircraft, consisting of 100 firm and 50 option orders for the 737-8/10 MAX models, have been completed, the carrier said. Safran shares are up 1.2% at 295.60 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0739 GMT - Safran will get a boost from Turkish Airlines' Boeing aircraft purchase, as the French manufacturer produces the Leap engine for the 737 MAX and equipment for the 787 plane, Jefferies analysts say. Turkish Airlines said Friday that it decided to purchase a total of 75 aircraft from Boeing, consisting of 50 firm and 25 option orders for 787-9 and 787-10 models with deliveries scheduled between 2029 and 2034. Negotiations with Boeing regarding the purchase of a total of 150 aircraft, consisting of 100 firm and 50 option orders for the 737-8/10 MAX models, have been completed, the carrier said. Safran shares are up 1.2% at 295.60 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0737 GMT - European pharmaceutical stocks fell in early trade after President Trump announced 100% tariffs on those companies that aren't building plants in the U.S. Swiss skincare company Galderma was the biggest faller, losing 3.55%, while Novo Nordisk shares are down 1.3%. AstraZeneca, Roche and Bayer shares were all down 0.2%. Tariffs are expected to start from Oct. 1.(ian.walker@wsj.com)

0732 GMT - China's new 2035 target of 3,600GW of solar and wind power capacity looks conservative and will likely be exceeded, Deutsche Bank analyst Gary Zhou says. He expects supply-side reform to remain the near-term focus, as restoring supply-demand balance in the renewable sector is key for investors. On the demand side, Zhou forecasts China will add 1,455GW of solar and 600GW of wind power capacities during the 2026-2030 period, well above additions in 2021-2025. DB's top renewables pick is GCL Tech, citing its low valuation and potential gains from solar supply reforms. Shares last traded at HK$1.27. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0723 GMT - Astro Malaysia's operating environment remains challenging, Affin Hwang IB analysts write in a note. The satellite TV operator's introduction of "Astro One" bundles has slowed subscriber churn, among factors, signaling some early success in product bundling. However, the average revenue per user continues to fall at a faster pace, raising concerns over the sustainability of revenue and profitability even if subscriber losses moderate, the analysts say. Astro's radio and television advertising expenditure also remains soft. This suggests muted consumer and business sentiment, as U.S. tariffs and global trade uncertainties weigh on spending, the analysts say. Affin Hwang IB cuts Astro's target price to MYR0.08 from MYR0.13, while maintaining a sell rating on the stock. Shares areflat at MYR0.14.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0718 GMT - President Trump's announcement of 25% tariffs on imported heavy trucks is light on detail, but Daimler Truck could suffer the most given current production in the U.S. and overall exposure to the U.S. at the group level, Bernstein analysts write. U.S. manufacturer Paccar could be the greatest beneficiary, it adds. Bernstein notes the tariffs apply from Oct. 1, so they won't impact the third quarter, where orders and volumes are weak for all manufacturers. It adds that further inflationary increases to truck prices will negatively impact all, especially with new truck prices currently considerably elevated after many years of price increases. It rates Volvo and Daimler Truck at underperform and Traton at market-perform. Volvo shares rise 2.1%, Daimler Truck shares fall 4.4% and Traton shares drop 2.1%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0700 GMT - The market likely doesn't believe the U.S.'s pharmaceutical tariffs will have real, significant bite in the end, says Citi Wealth's Ken Peng in a media roundtable. The head of Asia investment strategy notes President Trump offered an "escape" for pharma manufacturers, as they will be exempted from the tariffs as long as they start to build a plant in the U.S. While Asian pharma names fell after the news, Peng says: "If people really believed that large swaths of branded pharma would be tariffed at 100%, [the shares' movement] wouldn't be a 3%-4% correction... I think the market is calling it bluff." The new tariffs are also unlikely to hurt U.S.-China trade negotiations much, which he views as more important. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0652 GMT - The Philippine port sector's outlook appears positive, Maybank Securities' Germaine Guinto says in a research report. The ports' container volumes gained strong momentum in 1H and are poised to quicken in 2H, the analyst says. Growth is anchored by resilient domestic consumption and a pickup in trade activity, with foreign containers accounting for the majority of the ports' traffic, the analyst says. International Container Terminal Services, via Manila International Container Terminal port's dominant gateway role, remains best-placed to capture this momentum, the analyst adds. The brokerage has a buy rating and a target price of PHP590.00 on International Container Terminal Services shares, which are 0.6% lower at PHP495.80. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0648 GMT - Lower U.S. interest rates and a weaker U.S. dollar should support Chinese equities, UBS strategists say in a research note. Chinese offshore markets have historically outperformed in the six-month period following the first rate cut, they note. However, "despite ADRs and H-share historically performing better in a rate cut cycle, we retain our preference for A share for now as the potential impact from retail inflows could be more meaningful than foreign institutional flows," UBS says. Meanwhile, Chinese equities' valuations still look cheap to UBS after the recent rally. UBS likes mainland China stocks with exposure to AI themes, brokerages and high-dividend names. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0639 GMT - The impact of U.S. medical-device tariffs on European med-tech companies hinges on whether products for disabled people will be exempted, Bernstein analysts write. Products for disabled people are exempted from tariffs under the Nairobi Protocol and the effect of tariffs on the sector will depend on whether levies can override it, according to Bernstein. If applicable, chronic-care specialists including Coloplast and ConvaTec would be more exposed to potential levies than hearing-aid makers including Sonova, Demant, GN Store Nord and Amplifon, the analysts say. Another critical factor is manufacturing location. A recent U.S.-EU trade agreement caps tariffs on medicines at 15%, but it is unclear if similar agreements would shield medical technologies, Bernstein writes. Convatec fell 5.6%, Demant 4.8%, and GN 6.6% on Thursday. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0632 GMT - Lotte Chemical's earnings could start to recover in 2028 after completing its business restructuring, KB Securities analysts Wooje Chun and Yunju Song write in a note. The South Korean company is still reeling from the worst-ever global chemical-industry downturn, and is expected to complete its ongoing business consolidation by 2027, they say. The analysts note that global naphtha cracking center operators in Europe, South Korea, China and Japan shut down some of their facilities for four years until 2027 to address the oversupply. KB expects Lotte Chemical's operating loss to narrow to KRW287.5 billion in 2026. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0610 GMT - Global data-center demand is "insatiable," but demand in China is likely to grow at a faster pace than in the U.S., says Ken Peng, Citi's head of Asia investment strategy. Artificial intelligence and related sectors are among key industries driving global equities, he says at a media roundtable. Both the U.S. and China have companies sitting in the AI supply chain. However, China appears to be around a year behind the U.S. in terms of AI-related hardware and infrastructure, he says. This may indicate more room for growth. He reckons investors interested in this part of the AI supply chain could increase their emerging markets exposure. Meanwhile, those who prefer later-stage AI development could look into U.S. tech equities, he notes. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0609 GMT - Brunello Cucinelli needs to start a damage-limitation program as soon as possible or risk seeing a lasting blow to its reputation and valuation, analysts at Bernstein warn. Shares in the Italian luxury-fashion firm slumped 18% on Thursday on the back of a short-seller report alleging the company is selling products in Russia in a way that breaches EU limits. For Cucinelli, which consistently touts its ethical and humanistic values, the issue is a serious risk, Bernstein says, even if the potential legal repercussions are less certain. "Valuations are like reputations," the brokerage says. "They are built on predictability and reliability earned over time, but can be lost overnight." (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

data.title: The DAX Index Opens 0.52% Higher
data.description:
In Frankfurt, the DAX Index is gaining 121 points or 0.52 percent on Friday.

Leading the gains are Siemens (2.64%), Munich RE (2.19%) and Allianz (1.49%).

Top losers are Daimler Truck Holding AG (-2.44%), Infineon (-0.92%) and Deutsche Boerse (-0.85%).

data.title: FastPartner Signs Lease With Merz Therapeutics
data.description:
FastPartner AB :

  • SIGNS LEASE WITH MERZ THERAPEUTICS FOR 343 SQM

data.title: DAX Edges Higher
data.description:
The DAX in Frankfurt edged up 0.4% to trade above 23,600 on Friday, following yesterday's losses, despite the new round of tariffs announced by the US administration.

On Thursday, President Donald Trump announced that, beginning October 1, he will impose import tariffs of 100% on pharmaceutical drugs, 50% on kitchen cabinets and bathroom vanities, 30% on upholstered furniture, and 25% on heavy trucks.

Investors awaited this afternoon’s release of the Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) index, the Federal Reserve’s preferred measure of inflation.

data.title: China's 2035 Goal for Solar, Wind Power Capacity Seen as Conservative — Market Talk
data.description:
0732 GMT - China's new 2035 target of 3,600GW of solar and wind power capacity looks conservative and will likely be exceeded, Deutsche Bank analyst Gary Zhou says. He expects supply-side reform to remain the near-term focus, as restoring supply-demand balance in the renewable sector is key for investors. On the demand side, Zhou forecasts China will add 1,455GW of solar and 600GW of wind power capacities during the 2026-2030 period, well above additions in 2021-2025. DB's top renewables pick is GCL Tech, citing its low valuation and potential gains from solar supply reforms. Shares last traded at HK$1.27. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0729 GMT - Bitcoin steadies after sliding to a three-week low on Thursday as strong U.S. data reduced prospects of interest-rate cuts. This lifted Treasury yields and the dollar, causing bitcoin to dip below $109,000 and over $160 billion of crypto-market value to be erased, says Deus x pay's James Madden in a note. Less urgency for rate cuts has "dampened speculative appetite in the crypto market," he says. However, attention now turns to U.S. PCE inflation and revised University of Michigan consumer sentiment data at 1230 GMT and 1400 GMT, respectively. Softer data could enable bitcoin to rebound, although strong inflation could pressure it further, Madden says. Bitcoin rises 0.4% to $109,613, having hit a low of $108,715 on Thursday, LSEG data show. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0725 GMT - The Chinese yuan's recent stability is unlikely to be sustained over the longer term, Barclays analysts say in a research note. China's stronger yuan fixings around 7.10-7.11 over recent weeks have helped keep volatility low, but this is unlikely to be sustained due to near-term risks, including weak economic data amid tariff tensions. "Too much CNY stability or artificially low volatility could also be problematic" if it leads to complacency among exporters and importers on hedging activity, the bank says. USD/CNY is last at 7.14. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0710 GMT - EUR/USD's pullback from last week's high appears to have gathered considerable pace, based on charts, says Quek Ser Leang of UOB's Global Economics & Markets Research in a research report. The currency pair's fall over the past two sessions was the biggest two-session drop since late July, the senior technical strategist notes. Adding to the bearish momentum, the daily moving average convergence divergence indicator has turned noticeably lower, the strategist says. A key level to watch on downside is 1.1610 where two major supports converge, the rising trendline connecting May and August lows and the bottom of the daily Ichimoku cloud, the strategist adds. EUR/USD is 0.1% higher at 1.1679. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0701 GMT - The dollar turns lower after President Trump announced a new round of tariffs, including a 100% tariff on drugs by companies that aren't building plants in the U.S. as well as levies on large trucks and home goods, beginning Oct. 1. The dollar had jumped to a three-week high against a basket of currencies on Thursday after a string of strong U.S. economic data, including a sizeable upgrade to U.S. second-quarter GDP. This damped prospects of interest-rate cuts. However, Commerzbank analyst Michael Pfister says the U.S. economy's dependence on private consumption poses a risk if sentiment declines. Trump's "erratic trade policy is doing much to dampen consumer sentiment," he says. The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 98.409 after hitting a high of 98.605 on Thursday. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0623 GMT - Long-dated U.S. Treasury yields edge higher after some better-than-expected U.S. macro data. In particular, U.S. second-quarter GDP growth was revised to 3.8% from 3.3% on Thursday, with stronger consumer services and business investment. Among Friday's data, PCE inflation, the Federal Reserve's preferred gauge of inflation, is awaited at 1230 GMT. The 10-year Tresaury yield drifts 0.7 basis point higher to 4.180%, while the 30-year yield is up 1.4 basis points at 4.766%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0622 GMT - Realized and implied volatility are on a clear downtrend in Europe while government bond yields continue to drift higher, favoring carry trades, Societe Generale's rates strategists say in a note. They five-year area remains their sweet spot, with French government bonds, or OATs, looking "particularly attractive," they say. Spanish and Italian government bonds could also be favored, especially following a recent rating upgrade for Italy and a possible upgrades for Spain later Friday. Fitch Ratings and Moody's Ratings are scheduled to review Spain, they say. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0610 GMT - The Reserve Bank of Australia is expected to announce an unchanged official cash rate on Tuesday, in what might be a unanimous decision, says Andrew Ticehurst, chief economist at Nomura. The RBA's messaging appears set to pivot back in a much less dovish direction, compared with the communication it provided in August, he adds. Recent data has shown stronger GDP growth and low unemployment, while inflation looks to be heating up. RBA forecasts imply a trimmed-mean CPI rise of around 0.65% on quarter in 3Q and 4Q, while Ticehurst sees it tracking for a 0.95% increase in 3Q. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0557 GMT - U.S. Treasury yields are expected to decline and the yield curve to steepen, mainly driven by further interest-rate cuts by the Federal Reserve, say SEB Research strategists in a note. "We expect significant Fed rate cuts over the next 12 months to be the primary driver of Treasury yields," they say. They forecast the two-year Treasury yield to decline to 3.00% and the 10-year yield to reach a trough of 3.80% by mid-2026, resulting in a steeper curve, they say. "While the underlying trend points toward lower yields, be prepared for a volatile path." The two-year Treasury yield edges 0.6 basis point lower to 3.655%, while the 10-year yield is up 0.9 basis point to 4.182% in Asian trade, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0545 GMT - Eurozone government bond yields are expected to face structural, or longer-term, upside pressure, SEB Research strategists say in a note. Increasing bond issuance from Germany is among the factors pressuring yields higher, they say. Last week, the German Finance Agency raised fourth-quarter issuance plans versus last December's initial plan, and this pushed yields higher again. "The higher German fourth-quarter issuance plan, announced last week, pushed yields higher again, and the general supply pressure combined with an average 43 billion monthly reduction in Eurosystem asset holdings in the coming quarters continues to weigh on bonds into 2026," SEB strategists say. The 10-year Bund yield closed at 2.761%, according to LSEG data. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0521 GMT - The impact of U.S. trade policy on Japan's corporate profits, investment and wage growth could be limited if Japanese companies continue serving U.S. customers through their local subsidiaries, Capital Economics says. While BOJ data suggest higher U.S. tariffs have weighed on Japanese exports, the September manufacturing PMI subindex for new export orders indicates export growth is unlikely to weaken much further, notes Asia-Pacific head Marcel Thieliant. He adds that Japanese companies are already mitigating tariffs by ramping up production in their U.S. subsidiaries, with Japan's U.S.-bound FDI on track to hit a record high this year. However, Thieliant suggests that this strength in FDI likely reflects U.S. economic outperformance rather than trade-policy changes. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0513 GMT - Indonesia's GDP growth is likely to gradually soften over the next decade amid domestic political concerns and structural issues, BMI, a Fitch Solutions unit, says in a note. President Prabowo is targeting 8.0% growth by 2029 and high-income status by 2045 through fiscal reforms, expanded social programs and foreign investment. However, they note these would be insufficient to boost growth from Indonesia's long-term average of 5.0%. While Indonesia's long-term political outlook is broadly stable, there have been signs of democratic backsliding, including a controversial bill expanding military powers. "These factors, combined with a more uncertain geopolitical backdrop, could undermine investor confidence and slow investment growth over the long term," BMI says. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: Panasonic: To Delist Shares On Nagoya Exchange
data.description:
Panasonic Holdings Corp :

  • PANASONIC: TO DELIST SHARES ON NAGOYA EXCHANGE

data.title: FTSE 100 Trades Cautiously on New Tariffs
data.description:
The FTSE 100 traded flat to slightly higher on Friday, leaving it broadly unchanged for the week, after fresh tariff news from the US. President Donald Trump announced sweeping new levies, including a 100% duty on branded or patented pharmaceuticals starting October 1, unless firms are building manufacturing plants in America.

Imported heavy trucks will face a 25% duty, kitchen cabinets and vanities 50%, and upholstered furniture 30%, with all measures set to begin next week.

The UK pharma sector fell 0.8% on the news, with AstraZeneca down as much as 1.6% to its lowest since late July and GSK off 1%.

While both were among the FTSE’s worst performers, analysts note that the impact could be limited as major UK drugmakers already have significant US operations.

AstraZeneca, for instance, pledged $50 billion in US investment by 2030, underscoring the industry’s shift to expand stateside production and mitigate trade-related risks.

data.title: Iran says deal with IAEA hinges on lack of hostile action, including sanctions snapback
data.description:
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi said on Friday that Tehran would scrap an agreement to let the U.N. watchdog inspect its nuclear sites if Western powers reinstated U.N. sanctions.

A set of U.N. sanctions on Iran are due to be reimposed at 0000 GMT on Saturday after Britain, France and Germany accused Tehran of violating a 2015 deal with major powers aimed at preventing it from developing a nuclear weapon.

The International Atomic Energy Agency had been trying to rebuild cooperation with Tehran and resume inspections of its nuclear facilities after Israel and the United States bombed the sites in June.

But in a post on Telegram, Araqchi said a deal signed in Egypt this month “will remain valid as long as no hostile action is taken against Iran, including the reinstatement of United Nations Security Council resolutions that have been annulled".

He added: “Otherwise, the Islamic Republic of Iran will consider its practical commitments terminated.”

The Security Council is to vote on Friday on a resolution proposed by Russia and China to delay the reimposition of sanctions under the so-called "snapback mechanism" for six months, but diplomats said it was unlikely to pass.

The mechanism, enshrined in Security Council Resolution 2231, allows any party to the 2015 agreement with the U.S., Russia, China, Britain, France and Germany to unilaterally restore the U.N. sanctions that were suspended by the deal.

Tehran has long denounced the provision as a political weapon that undermines diplomacy.

data.title: U.S. Futures, European Stocks Rise
data.description:
U.S. futures for the S&P 500 were up 0.1% and futures for the Dow Jones Industrial Average increased 0.1%. Changes in futures do not necessarily predict movements after the opening bell.

In Europe, the Stoxx Europe 600 climbed 0.4% in morning trading. Alten rose 6.2% and Alstom added 3.3%. On the other hand, Brunello Cucinelli slipped 5.9%, and Teleperformance lost 3.5%. The FTSE 100 added 0.1%. Other stocks in Europe were up as France's CAC 40 gained 0.7% and Germany's DAX rose 0.3%.

The Wall Street Journal Dollar Index was flat at 95.75.

In commodities, Brent crude was flat at $68.61 a barrel, and WTI crude climbed 0.2% to $65.10 a barrel. The European benchmark price for natural gas, the Dutch futures contract TTF, was up 0.5% to 32.63 euros a megawatt hour.

The German 10-year Bund yield rose by 1 basis point to 2.781% from 2.775%, and the yield on the 10-year U.S. Treasury climbed by 2 basis points to 4.19% from 4.168%. Bond prices and yields move in opposite directions.

Stocks in Asia were down as Japan's Nikkei 225 index dropped 0.9%, and Hong Kong's Hang Seng declined 1.3%. China's benchmark Shanghai Composite fell 0.7%.

-This article was automatically generated by MarketWatch.

Write to Barcelona Editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: India's equity benchmarks set for sixth session of losses as IT, pharma stocks slide
data.description:
** India's benchmark Nifty 50 , Sensex down 0.5% each; set for sixth straight session of losses

** Fifteen of the 16 major sectors down; IT and pharma (NIPHARM) top losers

** Broader mid-caps and small-caps down 1.2% and 1.7%, respectively

** Pharma stocks down 2.1% as U.S. President Donald Trump announces 100% levy on branded and patented drugs

** IT stocks down 1.8% as Accenture's sluggish revenue outlook brings back concerns over weak growth for sector

** Benchmark indexes down 2.2% for the week, set for biggest weekly drop in nearly six months

** Among stocks, infrastructure major Larsen & Toubro jumps 3.4% on exit from loss-making Hyderabad Metro

** Tata Motors rises 2% as arm Jaguar Land Rover says some digital systems back online following the recent cyber attack

data.title: European steelmakers rise on prospects of duties on Chinese imports
data.description:
** Shares in European steel makers rise in early trading on Friday as EU considers levying duties on imports of steel and related products from China

** The European Commission plans to impose tariffs of 25% to 50% on Chinese steel products in the next few weeks, German business daily Handelsblatt reported

** The move could be a new method to protect domestic producers, as global overcapacity strains margins and makes investment in decarbonisation more difficult

** Steel makers in Netherlands, Aperam and ArcelorMittal are gaining 2.3% and 2.9%, while their German counterparts Thyssenkrupp and Salzgitter rise from 2.3% to 3.2%

** Meanwhile Sweden's steel mill SSAB and Finish stainless steel producer Outokumpu edge up 2.7% and 1.2%, respectively

data.title: BMW to recall over 196,000 US vehicles due to engine starter defect
data.description:
BMW is recalling over 196,000 vehicles in the U.S. as the engine starter relay may corrode, leading to overheating and short circuit, increasing the risk of a fire, the U.S. National Highway Traffic Safety Administration said on Friday.

The issue affects multiple models including Toyota Supra and 2022 BMW 230i vehicles, the U.S. auto safety regulator said.

BMW dealers will replace the engine starter, free of charge, NHTSA said in the notice.

data.title: Digital bank NOBA soars 27% in Stockholm IPO debut
data.description:
The share price of digital banking group NOBA rose 27% on its first day of trading on Friday following an initial public offering on the Stockholm bourse, valuing the company at 44.5 billion Swedish crowns ($4.71 billion).

NOBA, controlled by private equity firm Nordic Capital and Finnish insurer Sampo, was the second major Swedish financial services group to go public this year, with buy now, pay later lender Klarna making its New York debut this month.

NOBA's shares traded at 88.95 Swedish crowns at 0716 GMT, up from 70 crowns in the IPO which the bookrunners earlier this week said had been multiple times oversubscribed by investors.

NOBA operates across the Nordic region under the brands Nordax Bank, Bank Norwegian and Svensk Hypotekspension, and offers credit cards in Germany as well as deposit products in Germany, Spain, the Netherlands and Ireland.

($1 = 9.4429 Swedish crowns)

data.title: Tabcorp Upgraded to Buy from Neutral by UBS
Tabcorp Price Target Raised 65% to A$1.12/Share by UBS
